1: Psalm 100 carries the unique superscription mizmôr lĕtôdāh — "a psalm for/of thanksgiving." While many psalms express thanksgiving, this is the only one formally designated as such, likely used with the tôdāh (thanksgiving) offering of Lev 7:12-15. הָרִיעוּ לַיהוָה כָּל-הָאָרֶץ (hārîʿû layhwāh kol-hāʾāreṣ, "Shout for joy to the LORD, all the earth!") — rûaʿ (hiphil: to shout the glad war-cry): the same universal call as Ps 98:4. The psalm is a fivefold imperative sequence (vv1-4) followed by a double explanatory couplet (v5), fitting the shape of a procession into the sanctuary.
2: עִבְדוּ אֶת-יְהוָה בְּשִׂמְחָה בֹּאוּ לְפָנָיו בִּרְנָנָה (ʿibdû ʾet-yĕhwāh bĕśimḥāh bōʾû lĕpānāyw birnānāh, "Serve the LORD with joy! Come into his presence with singing!") — ʿābad: to serve/work/worship (the word covers both labor-service and divine worship — the same verb for Israel's slavery in Egypt and for Israel's worship of God in the desert, Exod 4:23: "let my son go so that he may serve me"). Service to God is not grudging obligation but joyful approach (bĕśimḥāh). Birnānāh: with ringing joy (the sound-word for piercing, glad song — Ps 47:1; 98:4).
3: דְּעוּ כִּי יְהוָה הוּא אֱלֹהִים (dĕʿû kî yĕhwāh hûʾ ʾĕlōhîm, "Know that the LORD is God!") — yādaʿ: to know (covenant knowledge — not merely intellectual acknowledgment but relational recognition). Yĕhwāh hûʾ ʾĕlōhîm: "YHWH, he is God" — the Elijah formula (1 Kgs 18:39: "the LORD, he is God!" shouted by the people at Carmel). הוּא עָשָׂנוּ וְלוֹ אֲנַחְנוּ עַמּוֹ וְצֹאן מַרְעִיתוֹ (hûʾ ʿāśānû wĕlô ʾănaḥnû ʿammô wĕṣōʾn marʿîtô) — the famous textual crux: the Ketiv (written text) reads wĕlōʾ ʾănaḥnû ("and not we ourselves [made us]") while the Qere (read text) is wĕlô ʾănaḥnû ("and we are his"). Both readings are theologically rich and defensible: the Ketiv refutes self-creation (Deut 32:6: "is he not your Father who created you?"); the Qere asserts covenant belonging ("we are his people and the flock of his pasture" — Ps 95:7; 23:1).
4: בֹּאוּ שְׁעָרָיו בְּתוֹדָה חֲצֵרֹתָיו בִּתְהִלָּה הוֹדוּ-לוֹ בָּרְכוּ שְׁמוֹ (bōʾû shĕʿārāyw bĕtôdāh ḥăṣêrōtāyw bĕtĕhillāh hôdû-lô bārĕkhû shĕmô, "Enter his gates with thanksgiving and his courts with praise! Thank him and bless his name!") — the processional sequence: gates (shĕʿārîm) → courts (ḥăṣêrôt) → the name (shēm). Each threshold is entered with its appropriate offering: tôdāh (thanksgiving — the verbal and cultic acknowledgment of grace received) at the gates; tĕhillāh (praise — the ongoing song of God's character) in the courts. Bārĕkhû shĕmô: bless his name (speak well of his name — the opposite of taking the name in vain).
5: כִּי-טוֹב יְהוָה לְעוֹלָם חַסְדּוֹ וְעַד-דֹּר וָדֹר אֱמוּנָתוֹ (kî-ṭôb yĕhwāh lĕʿôlām ḥasdô wĕʿad-dōr wādōr ʾĕmûnātô, "For the LORD is good; his steadfast love endures forever, and his faithfulness lasts to every generation!") — the explanatory kî-clause (because) answers why all the imperatives of vv1-4 are right. Three divine attributes: ṭôb (good — Ps 34:8: "taste and see that the LORD is good"); ḥesed (steadfast love — the covenant faithfulness); ʾĕmûnāh (faithfulness — reliable dependability). Lĕʿôlām: forever; lĕdōr wādōr: to generation and generation (both the unbounded time-scope and the household-transmission of the knowledge of God's goodness). This is the Psalm in miniature.
